questions.q: What is the primary advantage of SIMD (Single Instruction Multiple Data) execution compared to scalar execution?
options: SIMD instructions execute faster because they use more transistors
One SIMD instruction operates on multiple data elements in parallel, improving data parallelism and throughput
SIMD is always better for all workloads regardless of data layout
SIMD eliminates the need for out-of-order execution
questions.explanation: A single SIMD instruction (e.g., SSE, AVX) applies the same operation to multiple elements (e.g., 4 floats in SSE). This parallelism is explicit in the ISA and improves throughput on data-parallel workloads. SIMD is beneficial only for workloads with sufficient data parallelism and proper alignment.

questions.q: SSE (Streaming SIMD Extensions) registers are 128 bits. If each element is a 32-bit float, how many floats can be processed in a single SSE instruction?
options: 2 floats
4 floats
8 floats
16 floats
questions.explanation: 128 bits / 32 bits per float = 4 floats per SSE register. An SSE instruction like ADDPS (add packed single) processes all 4 floats in parallel. AVX extends this to 256 bits (8 floats), and AVX-512 to 512 bits (16 floats).

questions.q: What is vector alignment and why is it critical for SIMD performance?
options: Alignment is unnecessary for SIMD; only data size matters
SIMD instructions expect vectors to be aligned to their size (e.g., 16 bytes for SSE); misaligned access causes penalties or faults
Alignment is a compiler hint that doesn't affect runtime performance
Vector alignment reduces the size of cache lines
questions.explanation: SSE instructions expect 16-byte alignment, AVX requires 32-byte alignment. Misaligned access either causes performance penalties (hardware handles it slowly) or faults (on some ISAs). Properly aligned vectors enable single memory transactions and efficient caching.

questions.q: In AVX-512, each register is 512 bits. What is the vector width for 64-bit double-precision floats?
options: 4 doubles
8 doubles
16 doubles
32 doubles
questions.explanation: 512 bits / 64 bits per double = 8 elements. AVX-512 supports 8 doubles per instruction. This is wider than AVX (4 doubles) but maintains the same element width. Different element types (int8, int32, float) have different vector widths.

questions.q: What is the primary challenge when vectorizing code with data-dependent branches or irregular memory access patterns?
options: SIMD cannot execute any conditional code
Branches within SIMD loops cause all lanes to execute both paths, reducing parallelism
SIMD is incompatible with pointers and dynamic indexing
Vectorization requires all data to be stored in SIMD registers simultaneously
questions.explanation: When some lanes (threads in a vector) take one branch and others take another, both paths must execute with lane masking (like GPU warp divergence). This reduces efficiency. Irregular access patterns (e.g., via pointers) also prevent vectorization. Avoiding control/data dependencies is key to vectorization.

questions.q: What does the term 'reduction' mean in the context of SIMD vectorization?
options: Reducing the size of vectors to fit in cache
Combining multiple SIMD elements into a single scalar result (e.g., summing all elements)
Removing unnecessary SIMD instructions from a loop
Reducing power consumption during SIMD operations
questions.explanation: A reduction operation combines (reduces) all elements in a vector to a single result. For example, summing all 4 floats in an SSE register using horizontal add instructions. Reductions require careful implementation to maintain data dependencies and are common in scientific computing.

questions.q: Why is strided access (e.g., accessing every 4th element) inefficient for SIMD operations?
options: Strided access doesn't fit the SIMD vector width, wasting lanes and memory bandwidth
Strided patterns trigger cache misses on every access
SIMD only supports unit-stride (consecutive) memory patterns efficiently; strided access requires gather operations which are slower
Strided access requires more registers than unit-stride
questions.explanation: SIMD instructions expect unit-stride access (consecutive memory). Strided patterns scatter elements across memory, preventing efficient coalescing. Modern CPUs have gather/scatter instructions (VPGATHERDD in AVX2), but they're slower than unit-stride loads. Transposing data or rearranging algorithms for unit-stride is preferable.

questions.q: In vectorized code, what is horizontal reduction and why is it often a bottleneck?
options: Horizontal reduction is the process of accessing memory horizontally aligned with SIMD registers
It sums or combines elements within a single SIMD vector, but modern CPUs lack efficient horizontal instructions, making it slow
Horizontal reduction removes unnecessary instructions from a loop
It is always faster than vertical operations
questions.explanation: Horizontal reduction (e.g., summing all elements in an SSE register) requires intermediate shuffles and operations that are not as efficient as horizontal addition instructions (HADD). Modern CPUs have improved with VPHADD (AVX2), but horizontal operations are still a bottleneck. Restructuring code to use vertical operations is preferable.

questions.q: What is the difference between explicit SIMD (using intrinsics like SSE) and implicit vectorization (compiler auto-vectorization)?
options: Explicit SIMD is slower but more portable; implicit is faster but vendor-specific
Explicit SIMD lets programmers control vectorization via intrinsics; implicit relies on the compiler to detect and vectorize loops automatically
Implicit vectorization is only available for AVX; explicit requires SSE
There is no functional difference; the terms are synonymous
questions.explanation: Intrinsics (explicit) give programmers fine-grained control but require manual coding and ISA knowledge. Auto-vectorization (implicit) is automatic but requires aligned data, no branches, and clear data dependencies. Most production code uses a mix, with compiler pragmas guiding auto-vectorization.

questions.q: A scalar loop processes 1,000,000 elements with a peak throughput of 2 Gflops. If vectorized with AVX (8 doubles), what is the theoretical peak throughput if all elements are processed every cycle?
options: 2 Gflops (unchanged)
16 Gflops (8x improvement)
8 Gflops (4x improvement)
Depends on the specific CPU frequency
questions.explanation: AVX can process 8 double-precision elements per instruction. If the loop originally processed 1 element per cycle at 2 Gflops, vectorization to 8 elements per cycle yields 16 Gflops theoretically. Actual speedup depends on memory bandwidth, alignment, and register pressure. This assumes perfect vectorization efficiency.
